[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a quote again: 
 
"The balance of natural force is not lightly to be overset.  It is essential to the safety of the magician that he should conduct his operations in accordance with cosmic law; therefore he must understand the spiritual principle involved in every problem and work it out accordingly." 
 
That's from Dion Fortune's The Mystical Qabalah, her most influential book, and one of the books of hers that did an enormous amount to shape my understanding of magic and the occult.   

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

[IMAGE:1]

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

comments.content:
I have a crippling internet/computer addiction. It’s been a problem for ages, but has grown a great deal worse since March, with everything I used to use to keep it in check being removed, or worse, forced online. I’ve decided I really need to address it, so I spent a few months journaling on the issue, and found my internet use was a wall.

Simply put, my subconscious refused to discuss the root cause, preferring instead to redirect my attention elsewhere; and it kept getting very angry and defensive when I pointed out that I wasn’t doing whatever it was trying to say I needed to do before I could talk about internet use because I was spending basically all of my time online. Even just getting offline for long enough to do my journaling sessions was incredibly difficult.

I came to suspect I’m dealing with a very nasty astral parasite, and so on Tuesday I picked up my copy of LRM, flipped to the directions of the LBRP, and did the ritual. I repeated it Wednesday morning and when I journaled right after found that my subconscious wanted to address the problem of my internet use, for the first time. This lasted for about ten minutes, and then my internet obsession reestablished itself. It was fascinating seeing the rather sudden shift in my journaling, and although it hasn’t always happened while journaling, this pattern has repeated every morning since: a few minutes of clarity, and then my internet addiction reestablishes itself. It’s still here, but it’s a great deal weaker than it was when I started.

I also had the pattern you noted would occur with a crisis when someone starts banishing: every time I went online for anything, even if it was to do my job, I would get hit with horribly intrusive thoughts about how it was “clear banishing was doing nothing” and I needed to refocus on directly combating my internet use. The really weird thing is I know it’s working: I can feel the LBRP doing something.

Finally, on a mundane level I’m working to adjust my life to make having a computer unnecessary so I can get rid of it, since it’s clearly toxic for me, but this will take some time, especially given current circumstances; but easier to attain is the point where I can put it in a closet for a day or so. I’m already there; or I would be if I had the willpower to keep it there for more than a few hours. (Which is a massive improvement over where I was when I started banishing!)

1) I’ve had internet problems my entire life. I’ve lived with internet access quite literally since I was born, and have always found it far too enticing, and as I’m assessing my life, I’d say calling it addictive is fair. Is it possible I’ve had this astral parasite my entire life? How often does that sort of thing happen?

2) The other weird thing is that I’ve felt rather depressed and anxious about the current virus panic. I found it disturbing that so many people would lose their minds over it; and that so many of the things I did to keep grounded and stable are on indefinite hiatus. These emotions are still present after banishing, but vastly more manageable. You’ve mentioned a lot of people find that magic helps them deal with depression: do you think this might be because there’s a connection between astral parasites and depression?

3) I’m assuming the LBRP is going to be harder for me to perform, as well as harder on my subtle bodies, since I dove right in and skipped spending two weeks with the Qabalistic Cross; but is it normal for people who dive right in to find that it makes their hearts race and leaves them slightly out of breath? Oddly, this didn’t start right away, but it has occurred the last three times.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, you could have picked it up quite early, and I have no hard evidence about how common that condition is these days but I suspect it's fairly widespread.

2) Astral parasites are one possibility. Keep in mind, though, that regular banishing also cleans up your astral environment generally, and produces a less grubby state of mind that way.

3) That's ordinary fear. It'll peak as you dig down into the emotional knots that keep your addiction in place, and then fade away.

One other thing. When you've been doing the LBRP for a few months, add in the Middle Pillar exercise; when you've done those two together for a few more months, consider learning other magical rituals. Each working you do makes life more difficult for noxious entities and adds to your strength.

comments.title: Spagyrics
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Many thanks for these continued opportunities to run questions by you. I have a few regarding spagyrics:

1. I recently started working with Stavish’s The Path of Alchemy. He says that spagyrics can be shared with others but doesn’t directly mention children. I have a 1-year-old and a 3-year-old and would like to share the seven daily tinctures (I wouldn’t give them any more-advanced preparations) with my family to improve everyone’s general well-being. Is this typically safe to do so for children in low doses?

2. I noticed the plastic wrap between the lid and jar of one of my tinctures ripped, potentially exposing it to the metal of the lid. Is this a big deal or am I safe to continue with its creation?

3. While searching through previous Magic Mondays I found that you laid out a 10-year curriculum centered around the AODA system (9/30/19). In it, you suggested to begin working with spagyrics *after* going through DMH and then the Dolmen Arch, but not before doing so. While I’m interested in advancing in both magic and alchemy, I think alchemy takes a priority for me right now. So, is this advice specific to the AODA path, or do you generally advise against practicing alchemy until after obtaining magical competency?

Many thanks,
-Colter

replies.title: Re: Spagyrics
replies.content:
1) Frater Albertus mentions somewhere giving a spagyric magistery of chamomile to an infant with bad colic; the results were excellent. Ordinary spagyric tinctures are perfectly safe for children.

2) Replace the plastic wrap -- yes, that means you have to open the jar for a bit. It won't harm anything.

3) If alchemy takes priority, then restructure the practices accordingly. The curriculum I outlined was specific to the AODA system.

replies.title: Re: Spagyrics
replies.content: Thanks for this! That’s all good news. Since alchemy is a central component of the Celtic Golden Dawn, do you think it would make sense to work through the Ovate section and then stay in the Ovate grade while deep diving into alchemy? My thinking is that this would give me a basic structure and foundation to work within as well as a daily ritual, meditation, and divination practice as I study alchemy more deeply. Plus, staying in the Ovate grade would give my kids time to age before I get into the heavier magical stuff. Or would you suggest a different route?

replies.title: Re: Spagyrics
replies.content: That's a perfectly viable route, and the CGD system is set up to allow students to stop at any of the grades for as long as they wish. You'll find the alchemical preparations for the Bardic Grade initiation almost absurdly simple when you get to them, though!

replies.content:

Good evening JMG,

I have a Picatrix question, if you would be so kind: when bk3 ch1 describes Mercurial stones as "all stones shaped and inscribed" and minerals as "all stones that are raised up and used in noble works", this to me screams "marble"; do you agree with that assessment, or am I way off?

Speaking of Picatrix, which two or three books (if any) would you recommend to read alongside Picatrix to better understand it in context? My deepest appreciation for Magic Monday, it really is a high point of every week, and I glean a ton of helpful information from it every time.

Best,
Andrew

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Only if the marble has been shaped and inscribed, or if it has been raised up and used in a noble work. The rules are quite precise; if it hasn't had one of these things done to it, it doesn't qualify. (Substance alone is not enough.)

2) How's your background in traditional astrology? If it's at all weak, William Lilly's Christian Astrology should be your constant companion. Chris Warnock's Secrets of Planetary Magic is a good introduction to Picatrix-style magic. Finally, I'd recommend the scholarly books of Frances Yates, especially Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition, to give you a sense of the broader context of the work.

replies.content:

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yep. The Picatrix is not user-friendly and it's not intended for beginners; treat it as though it was an advanced, graduate-level textbook in which every word matters, and you'll have better results.

I’ve started doing some Qabalistic magic, and while I’m not yet up to the point where I can use magic to affect things on a large scale, I’m getting there, and so I’m starting to think through what I want to use it for. Since I’m another resident of Ottawa who’s sensing our city will have a tough time in the near future (there seem to be at least three of us, which I find a tad concerning), one of the major things I’m looking at is using magic to try to reduce the risks we’ll face. Some of it is unavoidable; but there are lots of things that depend on finer details which can be changed.

For a number of reasons, I suspect incompetent municipal politicians will play a role here, and this has gotten me thinking about municipal reform. This is a broad, amorphous enough concept that I don’t think my goal should be just municipal reform: it’s not focused enough, and it leaves too much to chance. Instead, I think I need to focus on one aspect of that broader task.

There are a number of ways that I think Ottawa’s municipal government could be improved, but one of the biggest ones is by breaking it up. Until 2001 the current boundaries of the city were a county; and trying to run a county as a city is a recipe for disaster. Thus, I think one of the biggest ways to improve government for us is to de-amalgamate the city and the rural areas around it. This is a nice, crisp, clearly defined goal, and so I think it could work for a target.

I don’t necessarily want to push for de-amalgamation in general, since other cities in Ontario probably ought to have been amalgamated when the government pushed through these reforms twenty years ago (ex: Toronto); and I’m also going to want to be careful about exactly what the target is for Ottawa, since the municipal borders before amalgamation were not ideal, and so it seems like reviving them would be counterproductive at best.

This leaves me with an avenue to explore, although exactly what the target ought to be is still something I need to spend a great deal of careful time considering, since the potential for causing other issues is something I need to keep in mind. I’ll of course do extensive divination on the topic before doing any rituals.

The exact goal to have is crucial for the occult aspect of it, but at this point I can ground my future workings by starting the process of getting people talking and hopefully interested in the idea of de-amalgamation: in other words, since my target is political change, the first steps need to be political action. I therefore ought to try to find groups pushing for de-amalgamation, and if they don’t exist, start them; start pestering the province, and start laying the ground work to make this into an issue for future municipal elections.

Does this sort of thinking seem reasonable/likely to get good results to you?

Political magic is far and away the most difficult and dangerous branch of magic I know of, and when one person tries to do it to shape the behavior of thousands of other people, major issues of scale also come into play. You might want to pick up a copy of Dion Fortune's book The Magical Battle of Britain and study it very carefully before going forward.

comments.title: Priapus and earth meditation

You mentioned that the Lord Priapus might like to receive sex as an offering so we tried it. Normally, our statue of Priapus lives in a little temple or grotto in the strawberry garden but for the magic sex rituals, we carry him to our flower garden by the house which is much more secluded. We live in the country and the garden backs onto a forest so it is very private.

replies.title: Re: Another assorted set of questions

The flames are quite traditional. In some pre-Hollywood magical writings, when you get to the point of the LBRP when you say "For about me flame the pentagrams," you're supposed to imagine the entire circle-with-pentagrams surrounding you bursting into flames -- specifically, the blue-with-yellow flames of burning alcohol. As for fear, that's pretty much a universal experience in this work. Once you push past it, the world becomes a bigger place.

replies.title: Re: Does human mean human
replies.content:
1) It's much more complex than that. First of all, many of their traces are currently underwater, buried in ocean sediments, or under ice. Second, archeologists are very good at not noticing things they've been taught not to notice -- watching them ignore traces of human settlement in North America before 12,000 NC has been quite an education. Third, we have very little knowledge about the nature of their technologies, and those could have been so different from ours that we wouldn't recognize them as technology.

2) The Polarian and Hyperborean cycles seem to have been genus Homo, but not H. sapiens. The Lemurian and Atlantean cycles seem to have been H. Sapiens.

3) "Human being" is a vocal noise used by some members of our species. Its meaning is very loosely fixed by usage; it has no inherent hard-wired meaning of its own. The only real answer to "what is a human being?" is to point at one and say, "a being like that."

replies.title: Re: Does human mean human
replies.content:
Lovecraft mentions the Theosophical vision of historical cycles in the opening paragraphs of "The Call of Cthulhu"; he knew a lot more about occultism than most people realize.

One of the great historical cycles, in this sense, is a period of something like ten thousand years. All but the first one emerged out of the ruins of a previous cycle, with some fragments of the older technology and culture flickering out around it, and gradually embraced its own distinctive set of ways of being human. So, yes, there's a long period of inactivity before and after, and there's a set of different approaches; each cycle also brings a different ethnic-linguistic group to prominence and, eventually, to temporary dominion over the world -- the Indo-European peoples in this cycle, the peoples of the old North Atlantic basin in the last cycle, and so on. (This is what was behind the somewhat clumsy Theosophical talk about "root-races.")

replies.title: Underwater
replies.content:
To echo John, during the last Ice Age, sea levels were something like 120 m (400 ft) lower vertically. That's a huge distance horizontally. As it warmed up, we went through several periods where sea levels rose sharply, then periods of stability until the present level.

Now since most earlier civilizations built their cities near the coasts, and we still do too, that would mean that ancient ruins of past cultures are miles out to sea now. Add to that, we tend to build at the junctions of rivers with the sea, where large amounts of sediment is deposited, its probably that even well built stone cities are under dozens or more feet of mud and silt.

There is evidence that the English islands were connected to Europe then, and that on the low lands now flooded there were people living.

Also, the Mediterranean and Black Sea both have experienced periods where their water levels were much lower. Both show evidence of fast flooding in the past that drastically raised their sea level. Again, cities before current recorded history are probably under deep mud as well.

Its probably our modern hubris to think that Cro-Magnum humans were so backward as to not be able to build cities of their own culture. That they lived in caves and in nomadic tribes as well, shouldn't preclude such construction, since a large portion of Homo Sapiens lived in similar conditions while Rome and Egypt were at the height of their glory.

Someday when or if aliens drop by, I expect that if we get to review their archaeology records of this planet's history, we will be in for a huge surprise that we aren't the first.

replies.title: Re: Underwater
replies.content:
This makes me think of some of the work of the urbanist Jane Jacobs (I believe in "Cities and the Wealth of Nations".) She makes a point to counter the mainstream view that humans developed farming in what we think of today as farmland. Her view is instead that humans clustered together in villages first, and farming technologies evolved out of these clusters of human minds in small "backyard" farms (one person drops some seeds by accident, others notice the result and try intentionally planting seeds, etc.)

Technology happens whenever human minds cluster together. Combine that with the view that applying our standards of technology to prehistory isn't the way to understand how advanced we truly were.

replies.title: Re: Underwater
replies.content: Colin Tudge, in his book Neanderthals, Bandits, and Farmers, points out that there's solid evidence for the deliberate planting and cultivation of hazelnut bushes in northern Europe by 40,000 BC. So I doubt anybody had to drop anything by accident; it was more a matter of "You know that trick with hazelnuts? Let's see if it'll work with those grass seeds."

replies.title: Body Paint
replies.content:
I had an amazing anthropology professor in College whose specialty was Central and South American Prehistory. He used to point out that you could get more calories by being a hunter/gatherer than you could with early farming yields.

He had a theory that agriculture in early C/SA cultures began with sunflowers and its purpose was for oils that were used in body painting. The more elaborate you could do your body paint the higher your social standing would be, as well as it would be a lucrative commodity to trade or sell. So being able to grow a good crop of sunflowers was something that developed outside of food needs.

Later, the skills you learned growing those, could be used to grow food crops well enough to make agriculture worth the effort. That lead to an increase in size of villages and then onto cities. And the rest is as they say, history.

replies.title: Re: Does human mean human
replies.content:
Have closely do you follow archaeology? Until very, very recently, it focused almost exclusively on "hard" remains: bones, metal, pottery, stone... it was as though nothing else existed. But most technology for most of human history has been biodegradable. Rope, leather, string, weaving, baskets, woodwork, thatch... these are all amazing technologies that greatly magnified humans' productive capacity. What good is a spear that can kill a mammoth, if you can't haul the carcass home? And because they rot, they're largely missing from the archaeological record. What we know about the ancient fiber technology of our own civilizations is vanishingly small, because with the rare and special exception of finds like Otzi, the remains just aren't there. And when the evidence *is* there, it usually takes (overwhelmingly male) archaeologists centuries to figure out what it is and realize how important it is. You could write a whole book on the finding --and dismissing-- of loom weights, by archaeologists.

If we have this much trouble recognizing and interpreting the traces of our own relatively recent ancestors, what chance would we have at the traces of culture and technology from 12000 or more years ago?

comments.title: Influence
comments.content:
Quoting from a previous Monday: “One of the basic maxims of magic says that the planes are discrete and not continuous….if you want to make something happen on the material plane, you have to use material plane means to do it. Magic is the art and science of causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will; when it has effects on the material plane, they're indirect, and happen because the change in consciousness influenced one or more conscious beings to do something on the material plane. ”

This maxim appears to contradict the principle of “as above, so below.” Thus, it voids the ground of being for synchronicity & symbolism. It also appears to invalidate a whole host of presumably mixed-plane events such as placebo/nocebo effects (astral-etheric-physiological), changes wrought in the body by social stress, vivid imagination, or strong emotion (astral-physio), behavioral changes effected by changes in affect, cognitive regulation, and sensory impacts via reading, teaching, memes, 12-step programs, art-drama-dance-poetry-music, and political rhetoric (mental-astral-material), It even challenges the validity of magic itself.

Unless there is a kind of ‘frictional contact’ such as the boundary between air and water or water and land where intentional, contained energy is transferred from one plane to another, how can changes in consciousness have any influence at all on the material plane? Harmonic resonance or quantam entrainment? If consciousness itself – or awareness, or focused attention – is the connecting medium, then it constitutes a substrate of beingness that acts as a kind of ‘force-field’ whence cross-plane events emerge. Changing it is the same as charging it past capacity, like a cloud that discharges polarity with lightning. But, if the planes are like a stack of disks one above the other, spinning at different speeds, none touching, no connection, then why should, how could, any influence leak or leap from one to the other?

replies.title: Re: Influence
replies.content: It's always tempting to take a general statement and reinterpret it in an arbitrarily extreme sense, but the result is always misunderstanding.  To say "the planes are discrete and not continuous" in no way implies that there are no connections at all between the planes; it simply means that there are meaningful divisions between them, and that if you want influences to pass from one plane to another, you need to know a little bit about how the connections work.

replies.title: Re: Influence
replies.content: Would it be correct to use the analogy that to say "the planes are discrete and not continuous" is akin to saying something like: The atmosphere is separate from the ocean, which is separate from the crust beneath the ocean floor, which is separate from the mantle beneath that, etc. They are clearly distinct "planes" with clear boundaries, but there is some cross-plane influence.

replies.title: Re: Influence
replies.content: That's a very good metaphor.

replies.title: Re: CGD LBRP
replies.content:
1) That's a typo -- the star should stay above your head.

2) You start in the center of the room. You go to the east -- as in, take as many steps as your room will allow. You go all the way around the circle, returning to the east. You then go back to the center -- as in, walk back to the center of the room.

3) You have to sit in a chair. Cross-legged postures cut you off from the earth currents -- which is appropriate for their kind of meditation but not for ours.

4) Put five minutes a day into just practicing with your imagination. Imagine objects, people, places. Practice makes perfect!

replies.title: Re: getting over a girl that you weren't exactly with
replies.content:
If you knew this girl in college, and it's been five years, I'm guessing you are still in your mid twenties. I (f, 40s) was also still thinking a lot about 'one that got away' from college at your age, despite having had a good relationship in the intervening years. I know what it's like feeling as if you are a weirdo for taking a long time to get over someone even if you are not actually communicating with them.

Having therapy for other, bigger issues loosened the problem although I never focused directly on it as it was minor. If you have other problems in the background and you can afford therapy, consider it. Maybe even for this if it's bothering you enough.

I am guessing JMG will recommend journaling.

Have you thought or written about what she and/or this time in your life symbolize to you, and how it all might resonate with events and people earlier in your life?

I would say there are two phases to dealing with something like this: understanding what it means on a deeper level, and seeing what you can do about that via available avenues (i.e. not dating this woman) - and making your current life exciting and interesting (maybe easier said than done depending what coronavirus restrictions are in your locality, but the occult and background reading is interesting and can be done at home).

Some people just take longer to get over these things than others. But if you can work through this, you may find it easier to see reasons why you were incompatible and are glad you are not with her. (Something I now see easily about people I used to wallow about.)

There is a lot of other advice in previous weeks about porn addiction, that seems to be successful for young men in particular. I've experienced short phases of it as a bisexual woman (like a few days, or a couple of weeks at most) and I don't think that's the same as having it an ongoing problem for months or years as a lot of younger guys have now. But as a first step I think it helps saying to yourself 'no', that you won't look at it when you get the urge, and remembering how you feel bad afterwards, and how annoying the waste of time is.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Because of all of the quotes you've started Magic Monday with lately, I bought Dion Fortune's The Work and Training of an Initiate, and Practical Occultism is on the way.

I've already read much of the former, and it has prompted a great many questions, of which the below are but a few. Sorry for all the questions, and thanks for having a look. For context, I'm working my way through the DMH, currently meditating on the Ogham fews to eventually get to pathworking.

1A. Is reaching Gwynfydd the same thing as becoming an Initiate, in Fortune's terms? I ask because...

1B. "The moon may be taken to represent the personality... whereas the Higher Self... is rightly symbolized by the sun." Is this just a difference in symbolism between Fortune's work and the DMH? I understood that creating the lunar current within oneself was how you start to get in touch with the Higher Self as part of the DMH practices. Fortune writes that the occultist path involves the work of preparing and controlling the personality such that the Higher Self becomes dominant. My first thought after reading Fortune's symbolism of the sun and the moon was that I was wrong with my earlier assumption: that the work in the DMH consists instead of preparing the personality. Unless it really is a different symbolism?

2. I fear not being conscious in my next life of what I now know in this life, or worse, of screwing things up or acting unethically in my next incarnation and accumulating bad karma. Especially after trying in this life to be careful about things. Fortune implies that you do pick up where you left off, I suppose, but I struggle to come to terms with it - I suppose it's impossible to somehow warn your next incarnation?

3. Fortune writes briefly about controlling the emotions, that rather than repressing an emotional response, you sublimate it. She writes: "An immediate reaction of resentment is transmuted into compassion and has its issue in charity". May I trouble you to say a bit more about how you do this, and how you practice doing so? Are certain negative emotions usually sublimated by particular positive emotions?

4. Unrelated to Fortune's book: while doing my daily SOP over the last few days, I've been struggling to deal with a particularly persistent type of mental chatter. Ordinarily I have to force away intruding thoughts, but lately the same thought about a co-worker keeps popping into my head, which keeps forcing its way in certain points during the SOP; to the point where I start to expect it to pop up at those points, making it even more persistent. It has taken on a life of its own. I keep trying to banish it, which helps, but it made me wonder what was going on, because it seems different than ordinary mental chatter. What might be happening here?

Thank you very much!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's a radically different symbolism. That's one of the traps in occult writings -- no two authors use the same symbols in exactly the same way. That's deliberate, to help counter the tendency toward fundamentalism.

2) Don't worry about it. Seriously, don't worry about it. You pick up where you left off, and the caution you're learning in this life will be a part of your character in your next life.

3) Let's use Fortune's example. You feel resentment. The first step is to accept calmly that that's what you feel -- don't get upset at yourself for feeling resentment, don't try to justify it, just be aware of it and accept that that's what you're feeling. Then put it in context. Why are you feeling resentful? Did the thing that you resent stomp on some childhood misery of yours, or jab some feeling of frustration or shame? Explore that. As you do this, you'll find that the resentment dissolves into another feeling, which you can then explore in the same way. It may well end up as compassion, but that varies; with me resentment usually involves old childhood issues being stirred up, and transmutes first into grief and then into a kind of acceptance: that was then, it was a long time ago, and I'm probably the only person alive who even remembers that it happened.

4) Does the thought involve something the coworker might want you to do? If so, it's possible that the thought is being projected at you from outside. Dismiss it and keep doing your practice.

comments.title: Affirmations One Year Update
comments.content:
A year ago you posted instructions for affirmations, and in the comments suggested that we could check in on the first Magic Monday of September with our results.

I chose one of your suggestions, "I recognize opportunities to prosper and succeed all around me." I have said it 100 times a day for the vast majority of the days this year (I believe I have missed about 20 days, most of them in the last month when I got cocky about having nearly finished and my practice fell apart. Lesson learned, and I'm back at it now.)

The results have been frankly astonishing. I started a new job at the same time as the affirmation, after two years of un/self-employment. To say that my finances were a mess would be a dramatic understatement. In addition to the morning 100 repetitions, I would say it any time I noticed myself stressing about money or anytime I had to decide what to do next in my job.

One year later and my boss frequently tells me he appreciates my work, I am current on all my bills, I have taken a much larger chunk out of my debt than I would have thought possible, and I have a one-month emergency fund built up.

I have been meditating and pondering to decide on the affirmation for this coming year. I have a problem with finishing projects. There are multiple reasons why, but one of them is that if I hit a decision point that I am unsure of, I tend to put it off and then the whole project languishes, sometimes for years. So I have chosen to focus on decisiveness. I adapted a sentence from Frank Channing Haddock's Power of Will into an affirmation: "I intelligently and forcefully decide all questions of life as they appear." Do you have any tweaks or suggestions? I think that "forcefully" might not be the best choice of words for everyone, but I have a tendency towards passivity so I think the energy in that word will help.

Thank you so much for the clear instructions! I have definitely been impressed by how well it has worked.

replies.title: Re: Affirmations One Year Update
replies.content:
As we are reporting back, and perhaps it is useful to be honest about lapses...

I am afraid that I stopped using my affirmations at some time in March when the coronavirus chaos started to hit. I had felt strengthened by them but one of the issues I was using them for felt swamped by what was happening. When I remembered about them again a few months later there didn't seem to be much point. Only at this very moment, as I have started writing this out does it feel like there is a point after all. I am getting the image of a tiny boat on a turbulent sea and that one can still give it a better chance.

replies.title: Re: Affirmations One Year Update
replies.content: That feeling of "there's no point to it" is a common trap, and it's also not true. Every repetition takes you closer to your goals; even if you use the affirmation irregularly, that's going to have much better results than not using it at all.

replies.title: Re: Affirmations One Year Update
replies.content:

In your place I'd replace "forcefully" with "firmly," because force isn't always useful in making a decision, but of course your mileage may vary.

comments.title: A Personal Pain Relief Experience
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

I would like to share with you a religious experience I had some years ago. I am looking to try to understand what happened to me, and so far the religious figures I have shared this with either give me the deer in headlights look or want to change the conversation.

I suffered from immensely painful migraines from the time I was a child, probably about 8 to ten years old, all the way until my earlier 30's when I started seeing a chiropractor who adjusted my spinal cord and took the pressure off the nerve endings at the base of my skull (throughout that time I refused to take opiod based pain killers, so there was little traditional doctors could do for me).

Anyway, one day I was having the worst migraine of my life. It lasted for over two hours. I did not understand at the time that sitting underneath a hot shower only aggravated the problem and inflamed the nerves (western medicine had kept me in the dark about that). I only knew that doing this temporarily made the pain go away, so I sat there for a couple hours looking for pain relief. I was in college at the time and due to running a particular program was committed to introducing a couple Buddhist monks before they began a sand mandala ritual. When the time came I stepped out of the shower, began to prepare to leave, and felt the old pain come surging back. I could literally feel the rhythmic throbbing in my skull.

I absolutely did not want to be there to help introduce the monks. I was in extreme pain, planed to be present for the opening ceremony, and then to crawl back to my dorm room and clutch the back of my skull for several hours in the dark until the pain went away.

And then the strangest thing happened. As the monks began to chant it felt like something reached into the back of my head and dialed down the pain. Within maybe 30 seconds all of the pain was gone. I know it could not have been a placebo effect because one, I did not want to be there, two, the experience terrified me at the time, three, I was not expecting any pain relief, and four, my own extremely conservative evangelical religious views at the time led me to believe that what just happened must have been caused by a demonic presence.

I tried to replicate the experience by listening to chant music the next time I had a migraine. The music was relaxing but no more so than any other kind of peaceful music. Whatever happened only correlated alongside the ritual that these monks were performing.

So what happened? Did I make contact with a being from a higher plane? Did the ritual send out a wave of calm that overwhelmed my inflamed nerve endings? Is this sort of thing common? Have you ever experienced tremendous pain relief from ritual?

I am sorry to inundate you with questions, but I am not sure what to do with this experience.

replies.title: Re: A Personal Pain Relief Experience
replies.content: The ritual was what did it. Buddhist rituals are generally intended to bless all sentient beings, and this one gave you the blessing you needed most at the time. Do you happen to recall which bodhisattvas or other Buddhist sacred beings the ritual was meant to invoke?

comments.title: Mythology book recommendations?

Hi JMG,

I would like to purchase some books on the Greek gods but I'm not sure where to start. Do you or your readers have any good recommendations?

replies.content: I've long been a fan of Bulfinch's Mythology as an intro to the Greek myths, though Edith Hamilton's volume is also good. After that, classic sources -- I'd turn to Hesiod's Theogony before the Odyssey, but your mileage may vary. Among scholarly sources, my favorite by a long shot is Walter Burkert's Greek Religion.

replies.title: Re: Mythology book recommendations?
replies.content: Perfect!  Thanks JMG!

replies.title: Re: Mythology book recommendations?
replies.content:
The best work I've seen is Jennifer Larson's Ancient Greek Cults. It's a scholarly source and it's essentially a comprehensive review of archaeological evidence, so it may or may not be what you're looking for.

Its real strength is that it's structured by God, then by location for each God, and then by time period for each location. Ancient Greek religion, like all natural religion, really had tremendous variation between regions and over time, and that gets left out in most sources that try to simplify the entire history of the Greek world into a single, coherent image.

It's also primarily archaeological, while most sources are primarily mythological. Myths are tremendously important, but I think that only tells half the story of how people actually relate to their gods, and the archaeology reveals more about the day-to-day practices of the average person.

Burket's book that JMG mentioned is probably a better archaeological overview, and I certainly recommend it as well. Burket does try to synthesize everything by time period, though, meaning that you're getting it from his particular perspective. Larson's book presents more raw evidence, contains things discovered since Burket's time, and give more detailed information for each of the gods it covers, so I think it's most valuable if you decided you want to focus on one or more specific gods and are trying to learn as much as you can about them.

replies.title: Re: Mythology book recommendations?
replies.content:
Hi Kimberly,
You might want to check out books by Karl Kerenyi, who I recently discovered. He is a Hungarian-Swiss scholar, writing from the 1950s into the 70's. He died in 73. I think he is more than a scholar, but a devotee. His books include The Gods of the Greeks; The Religion of the Greeks and Romans; Goddesses of Sun and Moon; Hermes Guide of Souls; Athene Virgin and Mother in Greek Religion; Dionysos Archtypal Image of Indestructible Life. He has other titles devoted specifically to Demeter and Persephone; Zuess and Hera; Asclepius; Apollo, and others. I think he has at least 15 titles.

I also recently discovered a classical source: Cicero’s The Nature of the Gods (and on Divination). Ovid’s Metamorphosis is also one of the better (albeit later) Classical retellings. There is also the classical title “The Library of Greek Mythology” by Apollodorus, which is succinct, but relatively comprehensive.

More recently, there is the French philosopher, Luc Ferry, who wrote The Wisdom of the Myths, How Greek Philosophy can Change Your Life. This book is a well-organized and excellent recitation of the Greek myths, and encapsulates, interprets and links them together cohesively. I do have a criticism, namely that Luc Ferry himself is an atheist, and he feels the ancient Greeks were proto or virtual atheists, as the myths don’t get into the afterlife (he overlooks the belief and devotion of Socrates and Plato, and didn’t bother himself with the development of Greek religion of the Neo-Platonists). There is another problem I have with Luc Ferry: He is a real privileged Schmuck. He is the Education Minister in France, and during the Yellow Vest protests in France last year, he publicly opined that the protesters ought to be gunned down in the streets… here is one source of many: (https://neonnettle.com/news/6143-french-minister-calls-on-police-to-shoot-and-kill-yellow-vest-protesters)... but I digress...

JMG, are you familiar with Kerenyi? What do you think of him?

replies.title: Diabetes

The traditional Japanese view is that nearly everything has a spirit, especially things that people have spent a lot of time with. The sword, for example, would get something from the swordsmith, but also from the person who carried it and practiced with it for many long hours.

comments.title: Magical Timing
comments.content:
Since your June post on magical timing (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/90808.html) I have been thinking about the correspondence between Moon phases and Sun phases (a.k.a. the seasons of the year). If my understanding is correct, aligning both Moon and Sun energies would make for most potent results, while mixing and matching them would allow for a bit more flexibility.

1. In your post, you discussed 2 phases of the Moon (New and Full) but left the others (First and Last Quarter) untouched. We of course have 4 seasons. Both New Moon and Spring are the time of new beginnings, that much is clear. You mention Full Moon is the time of closure and reflection, but that would correspond to Winter, does it not? What of Autumn, the harvest time? Is it possible to match Full Moon with endings in the sense of "time to reap what you sew", and leave Last Quarter the other side of endings (as in "time to end for good")?

2. Where do First Quarter/Summer stand here? Time of vigorous, indomitable energy?

3. I keep reflecting on the timing/meaning of Christian Passover: the first Sunday after the first Full Moon after Spring equinox. It is at the same time the Harvest/Fulfillment of the Old Covenant, and the beginning of the New one. At first, I thought the Sunday connection was obvious, "the Day of the Lord", only to realize that most likely the designation of the day was placed like that because of the Miracle of Resurrection. What would, then, be the actual meaning of Sunday in this?

4. This month we have the mirror image of the same timing: the next New Moon comes (September 17th) right before Autumn equinox this year. Am I correct to deduce it will be a good timing to start a new project that is meant to realize the fruits of something that has been in the back burner for a while?

Thanks in advance
CR Patiño

replies.title: Re: Magical Timing
replies.content:
Okay, to begin with, the quarters line up as follows:

New Moon - Spring Equinox
First Quarter - Summer Solstice
Full Moon - Autumn Equinox
Last Quarter - Winter Solstice

So they're at right angles to each other. Easter is at the point where the Sun is rising toward full strength and the Moon is falling from full strength, and it's a Sunday because Christ is a solar figure. And yes, that would be good timing.

comments.title: Learning magic without belief
comments.content:
I've read your Well of Galabes blog posts, and I found them interesting and to some extent convincing. I believe practicing magic could work.

But I am not sure if I really believe that it's doing something other than changing internal concepts in your mind, removing ways you are limiting yourself, whether in interactions with other people or in your own thought patterns. I guess I've become convinced that magic is potentially at least an effective therapy-like tool that can alter how you think and perceive things.

That's not your frame for doing magic, I think. Does it make sense to practice magic with the frame I've described? Or is this too much of "not believing in magic" for it to be worthwhile?

replies.title: Re: Learning magic without belief
replies.content: My recommendation? Adopt a genuinely skeptical attitude and don't try to decide in advance what magic can or can't do. Just say, "here are these practices, I don't know how or in what ways they work, and I'm good with that," and then see what kind of results you get.

replies.title: Re: Learning magic without belief
replies.content:
I had an even more skeptical attitude to start with: I was a diehard materialist, but I realized magic would work even from within that worldview: ritual was consciously-induced placebo; divination was tapping knowledge we don't know we know/introducing randomness; meditation was brain training.

Turned out that worked for a few months, and then I realized there's a whole other world here....

comments.title: Silence of the Gods
comments.content:
I missed the discussion about whether the Gods had gone silent or not, so to add a data point (also relevant to the synchronicity post on the other blog): a few days ago I asked Brigid to send me a sign if She wanted me to pursue a particular healing modality. I asked to see the sign at a specific place, thinking I'd spot a flyer or maybe overhear a conversation. Instead, a man walked by me wearing a cloth mask imprinted with a large Brigid's Cross and the logo for "Saint Bridget's Parish School." St. Bridget's Parish School is in a town a good 45 miles away. My maternal relatives lived in that same area, and I have many fond memories of the place. So, yeah, I'm finding the deities to be very active and communicative!

--Sister Crow

replies.title: Re: Silence of the Gods
replies.content: Doesn't surprise me at all. As far as I can tell, the only people whose gods are going silent on them are those who have gotten caught up in obsessive political passions of the "Orange Man Bad!" variety.

comments.title: Lack of Private Space, and Trinity Grove
comments.content:
Dear JMG:

I have a couple of different questions this week.

1) Many books and courses on operative magic require a private space dedicated to one's working. The requirements I've seen for this space usually call for: lack of distracting noise (soundproofing), sufficient space to circumvent around the room, temperature should be moderate, etc. My problem is that I lack this space in my home, no room meet the typical "Goldilocks" standard. Right now my magical oratory is an adjunct loft upstairs, but it's an oven in summer months, and the ceiling is at a steep angle (so no ritual movements without hitting my head or ducking). Is there a way I could set up a portable oratory space that I could set up and dismantle when not in use? I'd rather adapt my practice to my present circumstances than wait for the prefect space to arrive.

2) Once I finish and become competent in my studies with esoteric Christianity and Hermetic Qabalah I'm thinking of taking a more Green Ray approach to complement them. One option I'm considering is a Christo-Druidic order called Trinity Grove, led by Tau Blaise:
https://templarpriests.weebly.com/trinity-grove.html
I like their Avalonian and Mesopagan approach, do you have any thoughts concerning them? I ask you only because like Tau Blaise you're a previous member of OBOD, and wondered if you've heard of this order through the OBOD grapevine?

I appreciate in advance your thoughts and response. Have a blessed evening.

Sincerely,
Anon.

replies.title: Re: Lack of Private Space, and Trinity Grove
replies.content:
1) I've never in my life had a private space that I could set aside entirely for magic. I practiced magic in my bedroom for years, and now that we can afford a spare room as a study, the room where I practice magic is also the room where I write books and essays. It's never slowed me down in the least. Of course you can set up something and take it down when you're done -- most occultists do that.

2) No, I hadn't heard of Tau Blaise and his order, but I like what the website had to say about it, and he's quite straightforward about his initiatory origins, which is welcome. If I were twenty years younger and had the spare time I'd probably consider joining.

comments.title: Truth in Court, Time in Geomancy
comments.content:
Hi JMG & all--

1) Truth in Court
It looks like I will have to take someone to court to settle a dispute. Although he has been a world-class liar for the 16 years I have known him, I would like to have him tell the truth about the details of our dispute in court. I am thinking about doing a non-metallic talisman or gamahe to bring with me to court to encourage him along those lines--Can you point me to some resources on how to make one?

2) Time in Geomancy
One of my ongoing projects is to do a geomantic reading to find out when the next Juan-de-Fuca plate slip will occur(with tidal wave and real estate going into the ocean). It is already 77 years overdue. By doing a reading about every 6 months, I think that I can get a pretty good idea of when it will happen, if the readings align (so far, looks like its in a window between 2024 to 2027).
If the question is along the lines of "How many years until the next Juan-de-Fuca Plate Slip," can you confirm that the Quesited will be House 4, because the question is about land/real estate? Is there any significance in these questions of the Querent, Quesited, Judge and Reconciler from the Shield Chart?

Thanks for any information you can provide on this! --EG

replies.title: Re: Truth in Court, Time in Geomancy
replies.content:
1) Do you really want to try to override his will? That involves a lot of raspberry jam on your fingers...

2) It's a fourth house question, since that house governs all things underground (such as tectonic faults). Ignore the shield chart and focus on the house chart.

replies.title: Re: Truth in Court, Time in Geomancy
replies.content: To the OP, would a working to make the truth more apparent work in this case? That way, you aren't overriding his will, you're just giving people the ability to see through his lies. As long as you aren't keeping any big secrets yourself (not necessarily limited to just this matter, though!), the raspberry jam sounds less problematic that way.

comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I’ve been working on crafting an affirmation that will help me to take a more active role in my own life and get over my fears and frustrations. I’m settled on the first part of my affirmation, but I keep coming up with possible scenarios where the second part could backfire on me big time. Basically I’ve discovered that I have a major fear that if I am true to myself, I am going to stress, damage, or even destroy my relationships. So I’ve been going through my life prioritizing serving those I care about and neglecting my own needs. Obviously this is a frustrating way to live and I want to change it. How does this affirmation sound to you?
“I am confident. I respect and honor all aspects of my being.”
I know this is a simple affirmation, but it surprised me how long it took me to come up with a clear statement!
Many Thanks,
Silently Suffering 12th House Pisces Person
AKA Plutonic Pressure Cooker
